reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: The opening comparison table (hooks vs deployment scripts) gives the first-time reader an immediate, concrete decision framework — this is exactly what a low-AWS-experience reader needs before diving into configuration.
All three script types (LocalScript, BastionScript, LocalScriptWithBastionTunneling) are documented with working examples and clear when-to-use guidance. No union-type coverage gap.
The 'Common patterns' section is high-value for beginners: the side-by-side hook vs deployment-script migration examples make the tradeoff tangible rather than abstract.
The FAQ spans all three required categories — Stacktape-specific, underlying AWS service, and comparison/choosing questions — and answers are appropriately concise and standalone.

Failure semantics are called out per hook point (before-blocking vs after-non-blocking) in both prose and the table. The <Warning> about after-deploy hooks not re-executing during rollbacks is a genuine gotcha that would otherwise bite people.

No forbidden style violations detected: all Stacktape config is in <CodeBlock intellisense> with TypeScript tabs, no YAML resources blocks, no StacktapeConfig type import, no fenced TypeScript defineConfig blocks, no multiple commands in one bash block.
problems: The LocalScript example imports `$ResourceParam` but never uses it inside the code block. This will cause a TypeScript type-check warning/error in the code validator and may confuse readers who wonder why it's there.

Several LocalScript properties demonstrated in examples — `skipOnCI`, `skipOnLocal`, `pipeStdio`, `cwd`, `executeCommands`, `executeScript`, `executeScripts`, `assumeRoleOfResource` — are not present in the provided source files (`_root.d.ts` and `deployment-script.d.ts`). These are plausible but unverifiable; if any do not exist on the actual type, the examples will fail type-checking.
The `bastionResource` property shown in the BastionScript example is also unverifiable from the provided source files.

The claim that `stacktape deployment-script:run` 'hot-swaps the script's code (updates the Lambda function code) before invoking it' is specific and cannot be grounded in the provided sources. If inaccurate, it creates a false expectation about code freshness during manual re-runs.

Hook names `beforeDev`, `afterDev`, `beforeBucketSync`, `afterBucketSync` appear in the hooks table but are not verifiable from the provided source files. The `Hooks` type in `_root.d.ts` is referenced but not expanded. If any of these hook names are wrong, the table becomes a reliability hazard.

The <Info> callout claiming 'The `beforeDelete` and `afterDelete` hooks only run when `--configPath` and `--stage` are provided to the delete command' is a specific CLI behavioral claim with no source grounding in the provided files.
The FAQ answer 'scripts do NOT re-run during a `stacktape rollback` command — the CLI warns about this' asserts CLI warning behavior that is not grounded in any provided source file.
mandatoryFixes: Remove the unused `$ResourceParam` import from the LocalScript code example. The import appears in the `import { ..., $ResourceParam, ... }` line but `$ResourceParam` is never used in that block. An unused import in an `<CodeBlock intellisense>` block will fail the deterministic type-check pass.

Audit all LocalScript properties used in examples (`skipOnCI`, `skipOnLocal`, `pipeStdio`, `cwd`, `executeCommands`, `executeScript`, `executeScripts`, `assumeRoleOfResource`) against the actual `LocalScript` type definition. Add the LocalScript source file to the source list, or remove / replace any properties that do not exist on the type. Unverified properties that fail type-checking are a hard publication blocker.
optionalImprovements: Verify `BastionScript` property `bastionResource` exists in source and that the type accepts a string resource name. If confirmed, add a one-sentence note in the BastionScript section explaining that the bastion resource must already be deployed (or deployed in the same stack) for the script to run.

Soften or remove the specific 'hot-swaps the script code' claim for `deployment-script:run` if CLI source is unavailable. Replace with: 'Invokes the deployment script Lambda in AWS — useful for testing or re-running migrations without a full deploy.'

The hooks table lists `beforeBucketSync` / `afterBucketSync` — consider adding a note that these run only when using `stacktape bucket:sync`, since `bucket-sync` is a less-common command and new readers may not know it exists.

The 'Deployment script constraints' subsection lists Lambda runtime auto-detection and supported runtimes (Node.js, Python, Java, Go, Ruby, PHP, .NET) — these are useful but not grounded in the provided sources. Phrase as 'Supports the same runtimes as Lambda functions — see [Lambda packaging](/packaging/overview)' to avoid unverifiable specifics.

The `parameters` vs `environment` security note ('parameters are logged in CloudFormation events') is a useful production tip — consider moving it to an <Info> callout so it stands out for readers making that decision.

Add a brief note that `DeploymentScript` has its cost implications: it runs as a Lambda function billed per invocation and duration, so a 120-second migration at 512 MB is a small but real cost on every deploy. This aligns with the 'cost signal' requirement for opt-in features.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: The hooks-vs-deployment-scripts comparison table at the top enables the key decision in under 30 seconds — this is exactly what a deploying engineer needs.

Hook failure behavior table (blocking vs non-blocking per hook point) is precise and actionable; the Warning callout that after-hooks don't re-execute during rollback prevents a real production mistake.
All three script types (LocalScript, BastionScript, LocalScriptWithBastionTunneling) are covered with working code examples and clear when-to-use guidance.
Common patterns section covers the highest-value use cases (migrations, builds, cleanup) with both the hook and deployment-script approach shown side by side for the migration case.
Handler code snippets alongside config examples give the engineer the full picture — they don't have to guess what the Lambda event looks like.
`joinDefaultVpc` internet-access tradeoff is explicitly called out — this is a frequent production gotcha that most pages skip.
FAQ covers all three required question categories and has concrete answers that stand alone.
The `parameters` vs `environment` security distinction (parameters logged in CloudFormation events) is a genuinely useful tip.
problems: FACTUAL CONTRADICTION: The FAQ answer to 'Can I pass secrets to scripts?' says 'use $Secret() in either environment or parameters' — this directly contradicts the source JSDoc on DeploymentScriptProps.parameters which says 'Not for secrets — use environment.' An engineer following this advice could expose credentials in CloudFormation events.

Multiple LocalScript-specific properties are documented as facts (skipOnCI, skipOnLocal, assumeRoleOfResource, cwd, pipeStdio, executeCommands, executeScripts) but none appear in the provided source files. If any property name is wrong, engineers will silently misconfigure their pipelines.

The Lambda runtime list ('Node.js, Python, Java, Go, Ruby, PHP, and .NET') in the Deployment Script Constraints section is a concrete enumeration not backed by the provided source — the source only says 'auto-detected from file extension'.
The deployment-script:run behavioral claim ('does not update environment variables or other configuration') is specific operational detail not present in the provided source files.
The hook point names beforeDev, afterDev, beforeBucketSync, afterBucketSync are not verifiable from the provided source — the Hooks type definition is absent from the source files supplied.
The Info callout stating delete hooks 'only run when --configPath and --stage are provided to the delete command' is a specific CLI behavior constraint with no source backing.
mandatoryFixes: Fix FAQ answer 'Can I pass secrets to scripts?': Remove the claim that $Secret() can be used in parameters. The source DeploymentScriptProps explicitly marks parameters as 'Not for secrets — use environment.' Rewrite the answer to say sensitive values must go in environment, not parameters, and that $Secret() should only be used there.
optionalImprovements: Verify skipOnCI and skipOnLocal property names against the actual Hooks type definition before shipping — if wrong, engineers will silently get no-op config entries. If unverifiable from source, remove or add a caveat.
Replace the unsourced runtime list with 'auto-detected from file extension' and link to the Lambda packaging docs for the full list of supported runtimes.

Verify assumeRoleOfResource, cwd, and pipeStdio exist on LocalScript before listing them as 'Key properties' — these are high-confidence-sounding claims in a prominent position that engineers will try to use.

Remove or verify the Info callout about delete hooks requiring --configPath and --stage — this is a useful constraint if true, but presenting it as fact without source backing risks misleading engineers who test with other flag combinations.

Remove or verify the deployment-script:run 'does not update environment variables' claim — this is exactly the kind of subtle behavioral detail that burns engineers in production if it turns out to be wrong or version-dependent.

Add a short note that the executeCommands array runs each command sequentially, and that a non-zero exit from any command stops the sequence — this is the failure behavior engineers most need to understand when chaining build steps.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph is answer-first, self-contained, and within the target length.
Structure is highly scannable, with clear H2s, comparison tables, examples, API reference, and a broad FAQ.

reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: The decision table at the top of the page immediately answers 'which do I use?' — a first-timer can route themselves to the right section in under 10 seconds.

All three script types (LocalScript, BastionScript, LocalScriptWithBastionTunneling) receive equal treatment with working config examples, clear when-to-use guidance, and a bastionResource example that prevents common gotchas.

The database migration pattern is shown twice — once as a hook and once as a deployment script — with an explicit tradeoff explanation ('use hooks for convenience, deployment scripts for rollback safety'). This directly answers the highest-stakes decision on the page.
Handler code examples sit alongside config examples, making the deployment script section immediately runnable without requiring the reader to infer the handler contract.
The `joinDefaultVpc` internet-access tradeoff is called out in a Warning block, which is exactly the kind of gotcha that would otherwise cost a developer hours.
The FAQ spans all three required categories: Stacktape-specific behavior, AWS mechanics (CloudFormation rollback, Lambda limits), and comparison questions. The answers are concise and actionable.
problems: The comparison table labels the first column 'Hooks + scripts' and lists 'Runs on: Your machine or CI runner'. This is inaccurate for BastionScript, which runs on the bastion host inside the VPC — not the local machine. A first-timer reading the table will form an incorrect mental model before reaching the Scripts section.

The 'Shared script properties' subsection names 'ScriptEnvProps' as an interface that all three script types share. This interface name does not appear in the provided source files (_root.d.ts lists LocalScript | BastionScript | LocalScriptWithBastionTunneling as union members but does not expose a named shared base type). Readers may waste time searching for 'ScriptEnvProps' in the SDK.

The Debugging section only covers CLI log retrieval for deployment scripts. The section-level instruction says 'Where deploy-time state matters (rollbacks, cancelled stacks), describe the relevant CloudFormation stack states inline.' An after:deploy failure that triggers a CloudFormation rollback is exactly this scenario — the section should briefly explain what state the stack enters and how to distinguish a script failure from an infra failure.
optionalImprovements: Fix the comparison table: split the 'Hooks + scripts' column into 'Local/CI scripts' and 'Bastion scripts', or add a row-level footnote clarifying that BastionScript runs on the bastion host inside the VPC, not on the developer's machine.

Remove or de-emphasize the 'ScriptEnvProps' interface name. Either list the shared properties under a plain heading ('Properties shared by all script types') without naming an SDK interface, or verify the interface name against the actual exported types and only include it if confirmed.

Expand the Debugging section: after the debug:logs command for deployment scripts, add 2-3 sentences explaining that a failed after:deploy script puts the CloudFormation stack into UPDATE_ROLLBACK_IN_PROGRESS state, and that the rollback reason appears in the CloudFormation console events — this directly satisfies the section instruction about describing relevant CF stack states.

The hooks table Notes column contains prose guidance ('Build frontend, lint code', 'Run database migrations, seed data'). These read as examples, not constraints. Consider moving this guidance to a one-line prose callout below the table and leaving the Notes column empty or using it only for hard constraints (e.g., 'Only runs when --stage and --configPath are provided' for beforeDelete/afterDelete).

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: The hooks-vs-deployment-scripts comparison table is precise and immediately useful — columns cover execution location, trigger, failure behavior, and VPC access so an engineer can make the right call without reading further.

All Stacktape config examples correctly use `<CodeBlock intellisense tabs>` with TypeScript. No YAML fenced blocks, no StacktapeConfig type-import pattern, no fenced TS with defineConfig. Zero mandatory style violations.
The VPC gotcha (joinDefaultVpc causes loss of direct internet access → needs NAT gateway) is called out explicitly with a Warning callout, exactly where an engineer would configure it and get burned.
The before:delete vs after:deploy failure behavior distinction is accurately grounded in the source JSDoc and explained with concrete consequences rather than vague hedging.

The 'Database migrations — choosing the right approach' section in Common Patterns shows both approaches with equivalent depth: a working CodeBlock for each, plus a clear opinionated recommendation (deployment script is safer for production). This is genuinely decision-support, not just configuration documentation.

The FAQ spans all three required categories: Stacktape-specific questions (re-running a script without redeploy), AWS-service questions (Lambda execution limits, VPC connectivity), and comparison questions (deployment script vs Lambda function, hook vs deployment script). 10 Q&A pairs, all substantive.
Memory/timeout/storage defaults and limits in the deployment scripts table are accurate per the source: @default 10 for timeout, @default 512 for storage, 128–10,240 MB for memory, 900s max timeout.
Fenced bash blocks each contain exactly one command with no explanatory # comments. The copy-button trap is avoided throughout.
problems: Multiple LocalScript, BastionScript, and LocalScriptWithBastionTunneling property details are described but are not verifiable from the provided source files. This includes: the mutual-exclusivity claim ('You must specify exactly one of: executeCommand, executeScript, executeCommands, or executeScripts'), the cwd property, the pipeStdio property with default true, and the bastionResource property name for BastionScript. The source files provided cover only _root.d.ts (confirming the three script type names exist in the scripts map) and deployment-script.d.ts — no script type definitions are included.

The hook point names (beforeDeploy, afterDeploy, beforeDelete, afterDelete, beforeBucketSync, afterBucketSync, beforeDev, afterDev) are listed in a table with specific notes but the Hooks type is not in the provided source files. If any hook name is wrong, a reader configuring hooks will get a silent error.
'Logs from the script are streamed to your terminal in real time' (claimed for BastionScript) is a specific runtime behavior not verifiable from the provided source.

The claim that assumeRoleOfResource supports 'Lambda functions, web services, private services, worker services, multi-container workloads, batch jobs, and SSR frontend resources' is a specific enumeration not present in the provided source files.

The 'Local scripts with bastion tunneling' section states 'The environment variables injected by connectTo are automatically adjusted to use the tunneled endpoints.' This is a concrete behavior claim not verifiable from the provided source files.
optionalImprovements: Add a sentence to the Hooks section noting that the full Hooks type (including all hook property names) is documented in the API reference or a linked configuration reference, so readers know where to verify the hook point names and per-hook options like skipOnCI/skipOnLocal.

The 'Shared script properties' section references 'ScriptEnvProps' by name as the source of shared behavior — but that type is not in the provided source files. Either omit the type name and just describe the properties, or link to the API reference where it appears. Naming a type the reader can't find creates friction.

The comparison table shows Max timeout as '—' for Hooks/local scripts. Adding a brief parenthetical like '(no Stacktape-imposed limit)' would disambiguate — an engineer might read the empty cell as 'unknown' rather than 'unconstrained'.

In the 'Running scripts manually' bash example, the command uses --stage production without --region. Adding --region eu-west-1 or noting it is optional would match the deployment-script:run example below it, which does include --region.

The BastionScript section doesn't mention that a Bastion resource must be deployed before the script can run — this is an implicit dependency that could bite someone who adds a BastionScript in the same deployment that first creates the bastion. A one-sentence callout about ordering would prevent a common gotcha.

The deployment script packaging section mentions only two packaging modes (StacktapeLambdaBuildpackPackaging and CustomArtifactLambdaPackaging). The source type is LambdaPackaging, which may include other variants (NixpacksPackaging, etc.). If additional packaging modes are valid for DeploymentScript, they should at minimum be named; if not, a one-liner explaining why they are excluded would help.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph is answer-first, self-contained, and clearly distinguishes hooks from deployment scripts.
The comparison table is useful and gives readers a fast decision point between local hooks and AWS-side deployment scripts.
Stacktape configuration examples use <CodeBlock intellisense> with a TypeScript tab and avoid forbidden hand-authored YAML.
FAQ is broad and practical, covering Stacktape behavior, Lambda limits, VPC access, and choosing between approaches.
problems: Large parts of the page are not grounded in the provided source files. The draft states specific hook points, skipOnCI/skipOnLocal defaults, LocalScript execution modes, pipeStdio behavior, BastionScript streaming, LocalScriptWithBastionTunneling endpoint rewriting, assumeRoleOfResource supported resources, and CLI option shapes without corresponding source excerpts.

The page documents three script variants in depth, but the provided source only confirms that the top-level scripts map can contain LocalScript, BastionScript, or LocalScriptWithBastionTunneling. The concrete properties and defaults for those script types are not present in the supplied sources.

The comparison table includes unsupported deployment-script claims such as VPC access “via joinDefaultVpc” for all deployment scripts, local file access “No”, and “script must execute regardless of how the deployment was triggered (CLI, Console, or CI).” The provided source only says deployment scripts execute as Lambda functions and can be triggered manually.

Several CLI and Console-adjacent claims are unsupported by the supplied source files, including stacktape deployment-script:run option names, stacktape script:run option names, delete hook requirements, and the statement that deployment scripts run regardless of CLI, Console, or CI trigger path.

The page includes Lambda-specific details for deployment scripts that are sourced for DeploymentScriptProps, but then extends beyond the source by recommending NAT gateway configuration for VPC-connected deployment scripts and describing CloudFormation event exposure for parameters.

The API reference only covers DeploymentScriptProps even though a large share of the page teaches hooks and scripts. That may be acceptable for the route, but readers will not get reference coverage for the hooks/scripts material that dominates the page.

Some examples contain tunable values that are only partly explained. For example, database engine version '16', timeout 300, SEED_COUNT 100, and the bastionResource string are not consistently anchored in surrounding prose.
mandatoryFixes: Remove or source-ground every claim about hook points, script classes, script properties, defaults, CLI flags, bastion behavior, and assumeRoleOfResource. With the current source set, narrow the hooks/scripts sections to what _root.d.ts actually states or add the relevant type and CLI source files.

Correct the deployment-script sections so every Stacktape-specific statement is backed by deployment-script.d.ts: trigger values and failure behavior, packaging, runtime auto-detection, environment, parameters, memory, timeout, joinDefaultVpc, and storage are supported; unsupported operational details should be removed or clearly attributed only if additional sources are provided.

Do not claim Console-trigger behavior for deployment scripts unless console-app source is included. The draft currently says deployment scripts run regardless of whether deployment was triggered by CLI, Console, or CI, which is not grounded in the supplied files.

Add source-backed references for hooks and scripts or reduce the page scope. If the page remains “Deployment Scripts and Hooks,” include the Hooks and script type definitions in the source set so the page can safely document hook points, skip flags, LocalScript, BastionScript, and LocalScriptWithBastionTunneling.
optionalImprovements: Add a compact “Supported deployment-script properties” table focused only on DeploymentScriptProps, then keep hooks/scripts as a shorter orientation unless their type sources are available.
Move the strongest production recommendation into the first decision section: use deployment scripts for rollback-coupled deploy work; use hooks for local tooling and filesystem-dependent work.
Tighten FAQ answers that repeat earlier sections, especially hook-vs-deployment-script migration guidance, so the FAQ stays extractable without duplicating too much body copy.
Explain non-obvious example values near the code blocks, especially timeout increases and engine version choices, or omit values that are not necessary for the example.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is broadly aligned with the provided DeploymentScript source for deployment-script triggers, Lambda execution, timeout, memory, storage, runtime detection, parameters, and environment usage. The main problems are unsupported hook/script implementation details: the provided source only includes top-level `hooks`/`scripts` comments and the `DeploymentScriptProps` type, not the detailed `Hooks`, `LocalScript`, `BastionScript`, or CLI command schemas used by much of the page.

issues.statement: The draft documents specific hook points and CLI trigger coverage: `beforeDeploy`, `afterDeploy`, `beforeDelete`, `afterDelete`, `beforeBucketSync`, `afterBucketSync`, `beforeDev`, and `afterDev`, and says hooks are triggered by deploy, delete, dev, and bucket-sync.
issues.reason: The provided source only says hooks run scripts before/after deploy, delete, or dev commands. It does not provide the `Hooks` type, does not enumerate these hook point property names, and does not mention bucket sync hooks. Because readers may copy these property names into config, this is a Stacktape-specific configuration claim that is not grounded in the provided files.
issues.suggestedFix: Either remove the hook-point enumeration and bucket-sync claims, or narrow the wording to the sourced top-level behavior: hooks can run scripts automatically before/after deploy, delete, or dev commands. If hook-point details are required, include the `Hooks` type source in the source list before publishing.

issues.reason: The provided files do not define the hook entry shape or defaults. The only grounded hook statement is that each hook references a script defined in `scripts`; `scriptName`, `skipOnCI`, `skipOnLocal`, and their defaults are not visible in the provided source.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the sourced statement that each hook references a script from `scripts`, but remove the specific hook-entry property names and defaults unless the `Hooks` type source is provided.

hooks?: Hooks;

reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: The decision table at the top of the page immediately answers 'which do I use?' — a first-timer can route themselves to the right section in under 10 seconds.

All three script types (LocalScript, BastionScript, LocalScriptWithBastionTunneling) receive equal treatment with working config examples, clear when-to-use guidance, and a bastionResource example that prevents common gotchas.

The database migration pattern is shown twice — once as a hook and once as a deployment script — with an explicit tradeoff explanation ('use hooks for convenience, deployment scripts for rollback safety'). This directly answers the highest-stakes decision on the page.
Handler code examples sit alongside config examples, making the deployment script section immediately runnable without requiring the reader to infer the handler contract.
The `joinDefaultVpc` internet-access tradeoff is called out in a Warning block, which is exactly the kind of gotcha that would otherwise cost a developer hours.
The FAQ spans all three required categories: Stacktape-specific behavior, AWS mechanics (CloudFormation rollback, Lambda limits), and comparison questions. The answers are concise and actionable.
problems: The comparison table labels the first column 'Hooks + scripts' and lists 'Runs on: Your machine or CI runner'. This is inaccurate for BastionScript, which runs on the bastion host inside the VPC — not the local machine. A first-timer reading the table will form an incorrect mental model before reaching the Scripts section.

The 'Shared script properties' subsection names 'ScriptEnvProps' as an interface that all three script types share. This interface name does not appear in the provided source files (_root.d.ts lists LocalScript | BastionScript | LocalScriptWithBastionTunneling as union members but does not expose a named shared base type). Readers may waste time searching for 'ScriptEnvProps' in the SDK.

The Debugging section only covers CLI log retrieval for deployment scripts. The section-level instruction says 'Where deploy-time state matters (rollbacks, cancelled stacks), describe the relevant CloudFormation stack states inline.' An after:deploy failure that triggers a CloudFormation rollback is exactly this scenario — the section should briefly explain what state the stack enters and how to distinguish a script failure from an infra failure.
optionalImprovements: Fix the comparison table: split the 'Hooks + scripts' column into 'Local/CI scripts' and 'Bastion scripts', or add a row-level footnote clarifying that BastionScript runs on the bastion host inside the VPC, not on the developer's machine.

Remove or de-emphasize the 'ScriptEnvProps' interface name. Either list the shared properties under a plain heading ('Properties shared by all script types') without naming an SDK interface, or verify the interface name against the actual exported types and only include it if confirmed.

Expand the Debugging section: after the debug:logs command for deployment scripts, add 2-3 sentences explaining that a failed after:deploy script puts the CloudFormation stack into UPDATE_ROLLBACK_IN_PROGRESS state, and that the rollback reason appears in the CloudFormation console events — this directly satisfies the section instruction about describing relevant CF stack states.

The hooks table Notes column contains prose guidance ('Build frontend, lint code', 'Run database migrations, seed data'). These read as examples, not constraints. Consider moving this guidance to a one-line prose callout below the table and leaving the Notes column empty or using it only for hard constraints (e.g., 'Only runs when --stage and --configPath are provided' for beforeDelete/afterDelete).

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: The hooks-vs-deployment-scripts comparison table is precise and immediately useful — columns cover execution location, trigger, failure behavior, and VPC access so an engineer can make the right call without reading further.

All Stacktape config examples correctly use `<CodeBlock intellisense tabs>` with TypeScript. No YAML fenced blocks, no StacktapeConfig type-import pattern, no fenced TS with defineConfig. Zero mandatory style violations.
The VPC gotcha (joinDefaultVpc causes loss of direct internet access → needs NAT gateway) is called out explicitly with a Warning callout, exactly where an engineer would configure it and get burned.
The before:delete vs after:deploy failure behavior distinction is accurately grounded in the source JSDoc and explained with concrete consequences rather than vague hedging.

The 'Database migrations — choosing the right approach' section in Common Patterns shows both approaches with equivalent depth: a working CodeBlock for each, plus a clear opinionated recommendation (deployment script is safer for production). This is genuinely decision-support, not just configuration documentation.

The FAQ spans all three required categories: Stacktape-specific questions (re-running a script without redeploy), AWS-service questions (Lambda execution limits, VPC connectivity), and comparison questions (deployment script vs Lambda function, hook vs deployment script). 10 Q&A pairs, all substantive.
Memory/timeout/storage defaults and limits in the deployment scripts table are accurate per the source: @default 10 for timeout, @default 512 for storage, 128–10,240 MB for memory, 900s max timeout.
Fenced bash blocks each contain exactly one command with no explanatory # comments. The copy-button trap is avoided throughout.
problems: Multiple LocalScript, BastionScript, and LocalScriptWithBastionTunneling property details are described but are not verifiable from the provided source files. This includes: the mutual-exclusivity claim ('You must specify exactly one of: executeCommand, executeScript, executeCommands, or executeScripts'), the cwd property, the pipeStdio property with default true, and the bastionResource property name for BastionScript. The source files provided cover only _root.d.ts (confirming the three script type names exist in the scripts map) and deployment-script.d.ts — no script type definitions are included.

The hook point names (beforeDeploy, afterDeploy, beforeDelete, afterDelete, beforeBucketSync, afterBucketSync, beforeDev, afterDev) are listed in a table with specific notes but the Hooks type is not in the provided source files. If any hook name is wrong, a reader configuring hooks will get a silent error.
'Logs from the script are streamed to your terminal in real time' (claimed for BastionScript) is a specific runtime behavior not verifiable from the provided source.

The claim that assumeRoleOfResource supports 'Lambda functions, web services, private services, worker services, multi-container workloads, batch jobs, and SSR frontend resources' is a specific enumeration not present in the provided source files.

The 'Local scripts with bastion tunneling' section states 'The environment variables injected by connectTo are automatically adjusted to use the tunneled endpoints.' This is a concrete behavior claim not verifiable from the provided source files.
optionalImprovements: Add a sentence to the Hooks section noting that the full Hooks type (including all hook property names) is documented in the API reference or a linked configuration reference, so readers know where to verify the hook point names and per-hook options like skipOnCI/skipOnLocal.

The 'Shared script properties' section references 'ScriptEnvProps' by name as the source of shared behavior — but that type is not in the provided source files. Either omit the type name and just describe the properties, or link to the API reference where it appears. Naming a type the reader can't find creates friction.

The comparison table shows Max timeout as '—' for Hooks/local scripts. Adding a brief parenthetical like '(no Stacktape-imposed limit)' would disambiguate — an engineer might read the empty cell as 'unknown' rather than 'unconstrained'.

In the 'Running scripts manually' bash example, the command uses --stage production without --region. Adding --region eu-west-1 or noting it is optional would match the deployment-script:run example below it, which does include --region.

The BastionScript section doesn't mention that a Bastion resource must be deployed before the script can run — this is an implicit dependency that could bite someone who adds a BastionScript in the same deployment that first creates the bastion. A one-sentence callout about ordering would prevent a common gotcha.

The deployment script packaging section mentions only two packaging modes (StacktapeLambdaBuildpackPackaging and CustomArtifactLambdaPackaging). The source type is LambdaPackaging, which may include other variants (NixpacksPackaging, etc.). If additional packaging modes are valid for DeploymentScript, they should at minimum be named; if not, a one-liner explaining why they are excluded would help.

AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.

reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: The decision table at the top of the page immediately answers 'which do I use?' — a first-timer can route themselves to the right section in under 10 seconds.

All three script types (LocalScript, BastionScript, LocalScriptWithBastionTunneling) receive equal treatment with working config examples, clear when-to-use guidance, and a bastionResource example that prevents common gotchas.

The database migration pattern is shown twice — once as a hook and once as a deployment script — with an explicit tradeoff explanation ('use hooks for convenience, deployment scripts for rollback safety'). This directly answers the highest-stakes decision on the page.
Handler code examples sit alongside config examples, making the deployment script section immediately runnable without requiring the reader to infer the handler contract.
The `joinDefaultVpc` internet-access tradeoff is called out in a Warning block, which is exactly the kind of gotcha that would otherwise cost a developer hours.
The FAQ spans all three required categories: Stacktape-specific behavior, AWS mechanics (CloudFormation rollback, Lambda limits), and comparison questions. The answers are concise and actionable.
problems: The comparison table labels the first column 'Hooks + scripts' and lists 'Runs on: Your machine or CI runner'. This is inaccurate for BastionScript, which runs on the bastion host inside the VPC — not the local machine. A first-timer reading the table will form an incorrect mental model before reaching the Scripts section.

The 'Shared script properties' subsection names 'ScriptEnvProps' as an interface that all three script types share. This interface name does not appear in the provided source files (_root.d.ts lists LocalScript | BastionScript | LocalScriptWithBastionTunneling as union members but does not expose a named shared base type). Readers may waste time searching for 'ScriptEnvProps' in the SDK.

The Debugging section only covers CLI log retrieval for deployment scripts. The section-level instruction says 'Where deploy-time state matters (rollbacks, cancelled stacks), describe the relevant CloudFormation stack states inline.' An after:deploy failure that triggers a CloudFormation rollback is exactly this scenario — the section should briefly explain what state the stack enters and how to distinguish a script failure from an infra failure.
optionalImprovements: Fix the comparison table: split the 'Hooks + scripts' column into 'Local/CI scripts' and 'Bastion scripts', or add a row-level footnote clarifying that BastionScript runs on the bastion host inside the VPC, not on the developer's machine.

Remove or de-emphasize the 'ScriptEnvProps' interface name. Either list the shared properties under a plain heading ('Properties shared by all script types') without naming an SDK interface, or verify the interface name against the actual exported types and only include it if confirmed.

Expand the Debugging section: after the debug:logs command for deployment scripts, add 2-3 sentences explaining that a failed after:deploy script puts the CloudFormation stack into UPDATE_ROLLBACK_IN_PROGRESS state, and that the rollback reason appears in the CloudFormation console events — this directly satisfies the section instruction about describing relevant CF stack states.

The hooks table Notes column contains prose guidance ('Build frontend, lint code', 'Run database migrations, seed data'). These read as examples, not constraints. Consider moving this guidance to a one-line prose callout below the table and leaving the Notes column empty or using it only for hard constraints (e.g., 'Only runs when --stage and --configPath are provided' for beforeDelete/afterDelete).

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: The hooks-vs-deployment-scripts comparison table is precise and immediately useful — columns cover execution location, trigger, failure behavior, and VPC access so an engineer can make the right call without reading further.

All Stacktape config examples correctly use `<CodeBlock intellisense tabs>` with TypeScript. No YAML fenced blocks, no StacktapeConfig type-import pattern, no fenced TS with defineConfig. Zero mandatory style violations.
The VPC gotcha (joinDefaultVpc causes loss of direct internet access → needs NAT gateway) is called out explicitly with a Warning callout, exactly where an engineer would configure it and get burned.
The before:delete vs after:deploy failure behavior distinction is accurately grounded in the source JSDoc and explained with concrete consequences rather than vague hedging.

The 'Database migrations — choosing the right approach' section in Common Patterns shows both approaches with equivalent depth: a working CodeBlock for each, plus a clear opinionated recommendation (deployment script is safer for production). This is genuinely decision-support, not just configuration documentation.

The FAQ spans all three required categories: Stacktape-specific questions (re-running a script without redeploy), AWS-service questions (Lambda execution limits, VPC connectivity), and comparison questions (deployment script vs Lambda function, hook vs deployment script). 10 Q&A pairs, all substantive.
Memory/timeout/storage defaults and limits in the deployment scripts table are accurate per the source: @default 10 for timeout, @default 512 for storage, 128–10,240 MB for memory, 900s max timeout.
Fenced bash blocks each contain exactly one command with no explanatory # comments. The copy-button trap is avoided throughout.
problems: Multiple LocalScript, BastionScript, and LocalScriptWithBastionTunneling property details are described but are not verifiable from the provided source files. This includes: the mutual-exclusivity claim ('You must specify exactly one of: executeCommand, executeScript, executeCommands, or executeScripts'), the cwd property, the pipeStdio property with default true, and the bastionResource property name for BastionScript. The source files provided cover only _root.d.ts (confirming the three script type names exist in the scripts map) and deployment-script.d.ts — no script type definitions are included.

The hook point names (beforeDeploy, afterDeploy, beforeDelete, afterDelete, beforeBucketSync, afterBucketSync, beforeDev, afterDev) are listed in a table with specific notes but the Hooks type is not in the provided source files. If any hook name is wrong, a reader configuring hooks will get a silent error.
'Logs from the script are streamed to your terminal in real time' (claimed for BastionScript) is a specific runtime behavior not verifiable from the provided source.

The claim that assumeRoleOfResource supports 'Lambda functions, web services, private services, worker services, multi-container workloads, batch jobs, and SSR frontend resources' is a specific enumeration not present in the provided source files.

The 'Local scripts with bastion tunneling' section states 'The environment variables injected by connectTo are automatically adjusted to use the tunneled endpoints.' This is a concrete behavior claim not verifiable from the provided source files.
optionalImprovements: Add a sentence to the Hooks section noting that the full Hooks type (including all hook property names) is documented in the API reference or a linked configuration reference, so readers know where to verify the hook point names and per-hook options like skipOnCI/skipOnLocal.

The 'Shared script properties' section references 'ScriptEnvProps' by name as the source of shared behavior — but that type is not in the provided source files. Either omit the type name and just describe the properties, or link to the API reference where it appears. Naming a type the reader can't find creates friction.

The comparison table shows Max timeout as '—' for Hooks/local scripts. Adding a brief parenthetical like '(no Stacktape-imposed limit)' would disambiguate — an engineer might read the empty cell as 'unknown' rather than 'unconstrained'.

In the 'Running scripts manually' bash example, the command uses --stage production without --region. Adding --region eu-west-1 or noting it is optional would match the deployment-script:run example below it, which does include --region.

The BastionScript section doesn't mention that a Bastion resource must be deployed before the script can run — this is an implicit dependency that could bite someone who adds a BastionScript in the same deployment that first creates the bastion. A one-sentence callout about ordering would prevent a common gotcha.

The deployment script packaging section mentions only two packaging modes (StacktapeLambdaBuildpackPackaging and CustomArtifactLambdaPackaging). The source type is LambdaPackaging, which may include other variants (NixpacksPackaging, etc.). If additional packaging modes are valid for DeploymentScript, they should at minimum be named; if not, a one-liner explaining why they are excluded would help.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph is answer-first, self-contained, and clearly distinguishes hooks from deployment scripts.
The comparison table is useful and gives readers a fast decision point between local hooks and AWS-side deployment scripts.
Stacktape configuration examples use <CodeBlock intellisense> with a TypeScript tab and avoid forbidden hand-authored YAML.
FAQ is broad and practical, covering Stacktape behavior, Lambda limits, VPC access, and choosing between approaches.
problems: Large parts of the page are not grounded in the provided source files. The draft states specific hook points, skipOnCI/skipOnLocal defaults, LocalScript execution modes, pipeStdio behavior, BastionScript streaming, LocalScriptWithBastionTunneling endpoint rewriting, assumeRoleOfResource supported resources, and CLI option shapes without corresponding source excerpts.

The page documents three script variants in depth, but the provided source only confirms that the top-level scripts map can contain LocalScript, BastionScript, or LocalScriptWithBastionTunneling. The concrete properties and defaults for those script types are not present in the supplied sources.

The comparison table includes unsupported deployment-script claims such as VPC access “via joinDefaultVpc” for all deployment scripts, local file access “No”, and “script must execute regardless of how the deployment was triggered (CLI, Console, or CI).” The provided source only says deployment scripts execute as Lambda functions and can be triggered manually.

Several CLI and Console-adjacent claims are unsupported by the supplied source files, including stacktape deployment-script:run option names, stacktape script:run option names, delete hook requirements, and the statement that deployment scripts run regardless of CLI, Console, or CI trigger path.

The page includes Lambda-specific details for deployment scripts that are sourced for DeploymentScriptProps, but then extends beyond the source by recommending NAT gateway configuration for VPC-connected deployment scripts and describing CloudFormation event exposure for parameters.

The API reference only covers DeploymentScriptProps even though a large share of the page teaches hooks and scripts. That may be acceptable for the route, but readers will not get reference coverage for the hooks/scripts material that dominates the page.

Some examples contain tunable values that are only partly explained. For example, database engine version '16', timeout 300, SEED_COUNT 100, and the bastionResource string are not consistently anchored in surrounding prose.
mandatoryFixes: Remove or source-ground every claim about hook points, script classes, script properties, defaults, CLI flags, bastion behavior, and assumeRoleOfResource. With the current source set, narrow the hooks/scripts sections to what _root.d.ts actually states or add the relevant type and CLI source files.

Correct the deployment-script sections so every Stacktape-specific statement is backed by deployment-script.d.ts: trigger values and failure behavior, packaging, runtime auto-detection, environment, parameters, memory, timeout, joinDefaultVpc, and storage are supported; unsupported operational details should be removed or clearly attributed only if additional sources are provided.

Do not claim Console-trigger behavior for deployment scripts unless console-app source is included. The draft currently says deployment scripts run regardless of whether deployment was triggered by CLI, Console, or CI, which is not grounded in the supplied files.

Add source-backed references for hooks and scripts or reduce the page scope. If the page remains “Deployment Scripts and Hooks,” include the Hooks and script type definitions in the source set so the page can safely document hook points, skip flags, LocalScript, BastionScript, and LocalScriptWithBastionTunneling.
optionalImprovements: Add a compact “Supported deployment-script properties” table focused only on DeploymentScriptProps, then keep hooks/scripts as a shorter orientation unless their type sources are available.
Move the strongest production recommendation into the first decision section: use deployment scripts for rollback-coupled deploy work; use hooks for local tooling and filesystem-dependent work.
Tighten FAQ answers that repeat earlier sections, especially hook-vs-deployment-script migration guidance, so the FAQ stays extractable without duplicating too much body copy.
Explain non-obvious example values near the code blocks, especially timeout increases and engine version choices, or omit values that are not necessary for the example.
